##### 9.7.16.8.3. [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: `tcgen05.ld`](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-ld)

`tcgen05.ld`

Asynchronous collective load from tensor memory into registers.

Syntax

```
// Base load instruction:

tcgen05.ld.sync.aligned.shape1.num{.pack}.b32    r, [taddr];

tcgen05.ld.sync.aligned.shape2.num{.pack}.b32    r, [taddr], immHalfSplitoff;

.shape1 = { .16x64b, .16x128b, .16x256b, .32x32b }
.shape2 = { .16x32bx2 }
.num    = { .x1, .x2, .x4, .x8, .x16, .x32, .x64, .x128 }
.pack   = { .pack::16b }

// Floating point type load along with reduction :

tcgen05.ld.red.sync.aligned.shape3.num.redOp{.abs}{.NaN}.f32 r, redval, [taddr];

tcgen05.ld.red.sync.aligned.shape4.num.redOp{.abs}{.NaN}.f32 r, redval, [taddr], immHalfSplitoff;

// Integer type load along with reduction :

tcgen05.ld.red.sync.aligned.shape3.num.redOp.type r, redval, [taddr];

tcgen05.ld.red.sync.aligned.shape4.num.redOp.type r, redval, [taddr], immHalfSplitoff;

.shape3 = { .32x32b   }
.shape4 = { .16x32bx2 }
.redOp  = { .min, .max }
.type   = { .u32, .s32 }
```

Description

Instruction `tcgen05.ld` asynchronously loads data from the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory)
at the location specified by the 32-bit address operand `taddr` into the destination
register `r`, collectively across all threads of the warps.

All the threads in the warp must specify the same value of `taddr`, which must be the
base address of the collective load operation. Otherwise, the behavior is undefined.

The `.shape` qualifier and the `.num` qualifier together determines the total
dimension of the data which is loaded from the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory). The `.shape`
qualifier indicates the base dimension of data to be accessed as described in the
[Data Movement Shape](#tcgen05-data-movement-shape). The `.num` qualifier indicates
the repeat factor on the base dimension resulting in the total dimension of the data that
is accessed.

The shape `.16x32bx2` performs two accesses into Tensor Memory of the shape `.16x32b`.
The base address of the first access is specified by taddr and the base address of the
second access is specified by `taddr+immHalfSplitoff`, where `immHalfSplitoff` is an
immediate argument.

The destination operand `r` is a brace-enclosed vector expression consisting of one
or more 32-bit registers as per the value of `.shape` and `.num`. The size of the
vector for various combinations of `.num` and `.shape` is shown in
[Table 49](#tcgen05-num-shapes-ld).

Table 49 Various-combinations of .num and .shape

| .num | .shape | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| .16x32bx2 / .16x64b / .32x32b | .16x128b | .16x256b |
| `.x1` | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| `.x2` | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| `.x4` | 4 | 8 | 16 |
| `.x8` | 8 | 16 | 32 |
| `.x16` | 16 | 32 | 64 |
| `.x32` | 32 | 64 | 128 |
| `.x64` | 64 | 128 | NA |
| `.x128` | 128 | NA | NA |

The qualifier `.red` specifies that the reduction operation specified by `.redOp` is
performed on the data that is loaded across columns in each lane. The result of the
reduction operation is written into the corresponding thread’s 32-bit destination register
operand `redVal`. When `.red` qualifier is specified, `.num` modifier must be at least
`.x2`.

If `.abs` qualifier is specified, the absolute values of the loaded data are considered for
the reduction operation.
If `.NaN` qualifier is specified, the result of the reduction operation is canonical NaN if
either of the inputs to the reduction operation is NaN.

The optional qualifier `.pack::16b` can be used to pack two 16-bit elements from adjacent
columns into a single 32-bit element during the load as shown in the section
[Packing and Unpacking](#tcgen05-tensor-memory-ld-st-packing-unpacking).

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `tcgen05.ld` causes the executing thread
to wait until all threads in the warp execute the same `tcgen05.ld` instruction before
resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warp must execute the
same `tcgen05.ld` instruction. In conditionally executed code, a `tcgen05.ld` instruction
should only be used if it is known that all threads in the warp evaluate the condition
identically, otherwise behavior is undefined.

The behavior of `tcgen05.ld` is undefined if all threads do not use the same values of `taddr`,
or if any thread in the warp has exited.

The instruction `tcgen05.ld` is performed asynchronously and more details are specified in the
section [Memory Consistency Model for 5th generation of TensorCore operations](#tcgen05-memory-consistency-model).

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

`tcgen05.ld.red` is introduced in PTX ISA version 8.8.

Target ISA Notes

Supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

`tcgen05.ld.red` is supported on following architectures:

* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
  + `sm_103f` or higher in the same family
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
tcgen05.ld.sync.aligned.32x32b.x2.b32     {r0, r1}, [taddr1];

tcgen05.ld.sync.aligned.16x128b.x4.b32    {r0, r1, r2, r3, r4, r5, r6, r7}, [taddr2];

tcgen05.ld.red.sync.aligned.16x32bx2.x8.u32.max {r0, r1, r2, r3, r4, r5, r6, r7},
                                                 redVal, [taddr3], 16;
```

##### 9.7.16.8.4. [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: `tcgen05.st`](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-st)

`tcgen05.st`

Asynchronous collective store to tensor memory from registers.

Syntax

```
tcgen05.st.sync.aligned.shape1.num{.unpack}.b32    [taddr], r;

tcgen05.st.sync.aligned.shape2.num{.unpack}.b32    [taddr], immHalfSplitoff, r;

.shape1 = { .16x64b, .16x128b, .16x256b, .32x32b }
.shape2 = { .16x32bx2 }
.num    = { .x1, .x2, .x4, .x8, .x16, .x32, .x64, .x128 }
.unpack = { .unpack::16b }
```

Description

Instruction `tcgen05.st` asynchronously stores data from the source register `r` into
the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) at the location specified by the 32-bit address operand `taddr`,
collectively across all threads of the warps.

All the threads in the warp must specify the same value of `taddr`, which must be the base
address of the collective store operation. Otherwise, the behavior is undefined.

The `.shape` qualifier and the `.num` qualifier together determines the total dimension
of the data which is stored to the Tensor Memory. The `.shape` qualifier indicates the base
dimension of data to be accessed as described in the
[Data Movement Shape](#tcgen05-data-movement-shape). The `.num`
qualifier indicates the repeat factor on the base dimension resulting in the total dimension of
the data that is accessed.

The shape `.16x32bx2` performs two accesses into Tensor Memory of the shape `.16x32b`.
The base address of the first access is specified by `taddr` and the base address of the
second access is specified by `taddr+immHalfSplitoff`, where `immHalfSplitoff` is an
immediate argument.

The source operand `r` is a brace-enclosed vector expression consisting of one or more 32-bit
registers as per the value of `.shape` and `.num`. The size of the vector for various
combinations of `.num` and `.shape` is shown in [Table 50](#tcgen05-num-shapes-st).

Table 50 Various-combinations of .num and .shape

| .num | .shape | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| .16x32bx2 / .16x64b / .32x32b | .16x128b | .16x256b |
| `.x1` | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| `.x2` | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| `.x4` | 4 | 8 | 16 |
| `.x8` | 8 | 16 | 32 |
| `.x16` | 16 | 32 | 64 |
| `.x32` | 32 | 64 | 128 |
| `.x64` | 64 | 128 | NA |
| `.x128` | 128 | NA | NA |

The optional qualifier `.unpack::16b` can be used to unpack a 32-bit element in the
register into two 16-bit elements and store them in adjacent columns as shown in the
section [Packing and Unpacking](#tcgen05-tensor-memory-ld-st-packing-unpacking).

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `tcgen05.st` causes the executing
thread to wait until all threads in the warp execute the same `tcgen05.st` instruction
before resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warp must execute
the same `tcgen05.st` instruction. In conditionally executed code, a `tcgen05.st`
instruction should only be used if it is known that all threads in the warp evaluate
the condition identically, otherwise behavior is undefined.

The behavior of `tcgen05.st` is undefined if all threads do not use the same values of
`taddr`, or if any thread in the warp has exited.

The instruction `tcgen05.st` is performed asynchronously and more details are specified
in the section [Memory Consistency Model for 5th generation of TensorCore operations](#tcgen05-memory-consistency-model).

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
tcgen05.st.sync.aligned.16x64b.x4.b32               [taddr0], {r0,  r1,  r2,  r3};

tcgen05.st.sync.aligned.16x128b.x1.unpack::16b.b32  [taddr1], {r0,  r1};
```

##### 9.7.16.8.5. [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: `tcgen05.wait`](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-wait)

`tcgen05.wait`

Waits for the completion of all prior asynchronous `tcgen05.ld` / `tcgen05.st` instructions.

Syntax

```
tcgen05.wait_operation.sync.aligned;

.wait_operation = { .wait::ld, .wait::st }
```

Description

Instruction `tcgen05.wait::st` causes the executing thread to block until all prior
`tcgen05.st` operations issued by the executing thread have completed.

Instruction `tcgen05.wait::ld` causes the executing thread to block until all prior
`tcgen05.ld` operations issued by the executing thread have completed.

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `tcgen05.wait_operation` causes the
executing thread to wait until all threads in the warp execute the same `tcgen05.wait_operation`
instruction before resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warp must execute the
same `tcgen05.wait_operation` instruction.

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
Example 1:

tcgen05.ld.sync.aligned.32x32b.x2.b32     {r0, r1}, [taddr0];

// Prevents subsequent tcgen05.mma from racing ahead of the tcgen05.ld

tcgen05.wait::ld.sync.aligned;

tcgen05.mma.cta_group::1.kind::f16   [taddr0],  a-desc,  b-desc, idesc, p;

Example 2:

tcgen05.st.sync.aligned.32x32b.x2.b32     [taddr0], {r0, r1};

// Prevents the write to taddr0 in tcgen05.mma from racing ahead of the tcgen05.st

tcgen05.wait::st.sync.aligned;

tcgen05.mma.cta_group::1.kind::f16   [taddr0],  a-desc,  b-desc, idesc, p;
```

#### 9.7.16.9. [Tensor Memory Data Movement Instructions](#tcgen05-data-movement-instructions)

Data from the shared memory can be copied asynchronously to the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory)
using the [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: tcgen05.cp](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-cp) operation.

##### 9.7.16.9.1. [Optional Decompression](#tcgen05-optional-decompression)

Optionally, during the copy, a vector of 4-bit and 6-bit
custom floating point types can be decompressed into 8-bit types.

###### 9.7.16.9.1.1. [Decompression of 4-bit floating point to 8-bit type](#tcgen05-optional-decompression-4bit-8bit)

A contiguous set of 16 elements of 4-bits each followed by 8 bytes of padding can be converted
into 16 elements of 8-bits each as shown in [Figure 194](#tcgen05-decompression-4b8b).



[Figure: tcgen05-decompression-4b8b]
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates the decompression operation that occurs when copying data from Shared Memory to Tensor Memory using the tcgen05.cp instruction. Specifically, it shows how 4-bit packed data (.b4x16_p64 format) stored in Shared Memory is decompressed/expanded into 8-bit data (.b8x16 format) in Tensor Memory. Each 4-bit element is unpacked into an 8-bit element, effectively doubling the number of bytes used to represent the same data.

COMPONENTS AND LAYOUT:

Top Section - Shared Memory:
- Label: "Shared Memory" appears at the top left
- Format label: ".b4x16_p64" appears above the right-center portion of the memory bar
- Represented as a single horizontal bar containing many small cells (columns)
- The bar is narrower/shorter in total visual width compared to the Tensor Memory bar below, reflecting the packed/compressed nature of the 4-bit data
- The cells are colored in a repeating pattern of colors: green, white/light, blue/light blue, yellow/orange, peach/salmon, green, white, yellow, and other pastel shades
- The colored region occupies roughly the left half to two-thirds of the bar, with the right portion appearing mostly white/empty (uncolored cells)
- Black vertical dividers separate individual cells
- The colored cells represent the actual 4-bit packed data elements

Arrow (Data Flow):
- A solid blue downward-pointing arrow is positioned in the center between the two memory bars
- Label: "tcgen05.cp" appears to the right of the arrow
- This indicates the PTX instruction that performs the copy and decompression operation

Bottom Section - Tensor Memory:
- Label: "Tensor Memory" appears at the bottom left
- Format label: ".b8x16" appears below the center of the memory bar
- Represented as a single horizontal bar that is visually wider/longer than the Shared Memory bar above
- The bar contains more cells than the Shared Memory bar, and ALL cells across the entire bar are colored (no white/empty cells visible)
- The same color pattern is present but now spread across the full width of the bar: green, blue/light blue, yellow/orange, peach/salmon, green, blue, yellow, peach, and repeating pastel colors
- The colors correspond to the same data elements as in Shared Memory but each element now occupies a wider (8-bit) cell
- Black vertical dividers separate individual cells

NUMERICAL VALUES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Source format: .b4x16_p64 — 4-bit elements, 16 elements per group, packed with 64-bit packing
- Destination format: .b8x16 — 8-bit elements, 16 elements per group
- The decompression ratio is 2:1 in terms of bits per element (4-bit to 8-bit), meaning the data expands to twice the storage size
- The Tensor Memory bar visually appears approximately twice as wide as the populated (colored) portion of the Shared Memory bar, consistent with the 2x expansion

DATA FLOW AND ORGANIZATION PATTERN:
- Data flows from top (Shared Memory) to bottom (Tensor Memory) via the tcgen05.cp instruction
- Each 4-bit element in the packed Shared Memory representation is decompressed into a corresponding 8-bit element in Tensor Memory
- The color coding is consistent between source and destination: matching colors in both bars represent the same logical data elements, demonstrating that the values are preserved but stored in a wider format
- The packed source (.b4x16_p64) has elements stored compactly; the destination (.b8x16) has each element zero-extended or sign-extended to 8 bits
- The overall data pattern (color sequence) is maintained across the decompression, confirming element-to-element correspondence

TEXT LABELS SUMMARY:
- "Shared Memory" — identifies the source memory type
- ".b4x16_p64" — identifies the source data format (4-bit elements, 16 per tile, 64-bit packed)
- "tcgen05.cp" — the PTX instruction performing the decompression copy
- "Tensor Memory" — identifies the destination memory type
- ".b8x16" — identifies the destination data format (8-bit elements, 16 per tile)


Figure 194 Decompression from 4-bit to 8-bit

The individual 4-bit to 8-bit decompression would look like as shown in [Figure 195](#tcgen05-decompression-4b8b-individual).



[Figure: tcgen05-decompression-4b8b-individual]
Individual Decompression from 4-bit to 8-bit

CONCEPT:
This figure illustrates how a single 4-bit floating point value is decompressed/expanded into an 8-bit floating point value. It shows the bit-field mapping between a compact 4-bit representation and its expanded 8-bit form.

TOP ROW - 8-bit Source Register (Input):
A row of 8 individual bit cells numbered from right to left: 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0
These represent bit positions 0 through 7 of an 8-bit value.
All cells are shown as plain white/empty boxes.

MIDDLE SECTION - 4-bit Field Extraction:
Three labeled fields are extracted from specific bit positions of the 8-bit input:
- S (Sign bit): shown in green/light green background, occupies 1 bit
- E2 (Exponent field): shown in yellow/light yellow background, occupies 2 bits
- M1 (Mantissa field): shown in orange/light orange background, occupies 1 bit

These three fields together form the 4-bit compressed representation (1 + 2 + 1 = 4 bits total).

ARROW:
A large solid blue downward-pointing arrow indicates the decompression transformation direction, from the 4-bit extracted fields to the 8-bit output.

BOTTOM ROW - 8-bit Output (Decompressed Result):
A row of 8 bit cells representing the expanded 8-bit value, laid out from left to right:
- Bit 7: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 6: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 5: S (green background, sign bit placed here)
- Bit 4-3: E2 (yellow background, exponent bits placed here)
- Bit 2: M1 (orange background, mantissa bit placed here)
- Bit 1: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)
- Bit 0: 0 (plain white, zero-filled)

DATA FLOW / MAPPING PATTERN:
The 4-bit fields (S, E2, M1) are extracted from the input 8-bit register and then repositioned within an 8-bit output word. The upper 2 bits (positions 7-6) are zero-filled, the lower 2 bits (positions 1-0) are zero-filled, and the S, E2, M1 fields are placed in the middle bit positions (5, 4-3, 2) of the 8-bit output. This represents a format conversion where the compact 4-bit float fields are mapped into corresponding fields of an 8-bit float format with zero padding on both ends.


Figure 195 Individual decompression from 4-bit to 8-bit

This figure illustrates the decompression operation from 6-bit packed data format to 8-bit data format, specifically showing how the tcgen05.cp instruction copies and decompresses data from Shared Memory into Tensor Memory on NVIDIA GPUs.

The figure contains two horizontal memory layout bars arranged vertically, with a downward arrow between them indicating the direction of data flow (copy/decompression operation).

Label: "Shared Memory" (positioned at top-left)

Description: A horizontal bar representing a region of shared memory containing 6-bit packed data. The bar is visually shorter/narrower than the Tensor Memory bar below it, reflecting that 6-bit data is more compact. The bar contains a repeating pattern of colored segments (green, blue/light blue, yellow/tan, pink/salmon, and white segments) separated by thin black dividers. The colored segments appear in groups, representing packed 6-bit values. The rightmost portion of the shared memory bar contains predominantly white/empty-looking segments, which likely represent padding bits associated with the .p32 (pad to 32-bit) format.

Label: "tcgen05.cp" (positioned to the right of the arrow)

This bar is visually longer/wider than the Shared Memory bar above it, reflecting that 8-bit data occupies more space than 6-bit data. The bar contains a similar repeating pattern of colored segments (green, blue/light blue, yellow/tan, pink/salmon) separated by thin black dividers. The pattern extends further to the right compared to the shared memory bar, and notably there are no white/padding segments — all segments appear to contain valid colored data values.

COLOR PATTERN AND DATA ORGANIZATION:

- Green segments
- Light blue/periwinkle segments

These colors repeat in a consistent cyclic pattern across both bars, allowing visual correspondence between the compressed source data and the decompressed destination data.

The 6-bit packed values (with padding) in shared memory are decompressed/unpacked into full 8-bit values stored in tensor memory. The tensor memory bar is wider than the shared memory bar, visually confirming the expansion in data size from 6-bit to 8-bit representation.

The individual 6-bit to 8-bit decompression for types `E3M2` and `E2M3` is shown in
[Figure 197](#tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual1) and [Figure 198](#tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual2)
respectively.



[Figure: tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual1]
This figure illustrates the individual decompression process from a 6-bit E3M2 floating-point format to an 8-bit format.

CONCEPT:
The figure shows how a 6-bit E3M2 floating-point value is expanded/decompressed into an 8-bit representation by zero-extending the upper bits.

LAYOUT DESCRIPTION:

Row 1 - Bit Position Header:
A row of boxes labeled with bit positions from left to right: 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0
These represent the 8 bit positions of the output byte.

Row 2 - 6-bit E3M2 Source Format:
This row is offset to the right, covering only bit positions 5 down to 0 (6 bits total).
The fields are:
- S (Sign bit): occupies bit position 5, colored green/yellow
- E3 (3-bit Exponent): occupies bit positions 4, 3, 2, colored blue/teal
- M2 (2-bit Mantissa): occupies bit positions 1, 0, colored yellow/tan

Row 3 - 8-bit Output Format:
This row spans all 8 bit positions (7 down to 0).
The fields are:
- Bit 7: contains value 0 (fixed zero), colored white/gray
- Bit 6: contains value 0 (fixed zero), colored white/gray
- S (Sign bit): occupies bit position 5, colored green/yellow — same as source
- E3 (3-bit Exponent): occupies bit positions 4, 3, 2, colored blue/teal — same as source
- M2 (2-bit Mantissa): occupies bit positions 1, 0, colored yellow/tan — same as source

DATA FLOW / PATTERN:
The 6-bit E3M2 value (consisting of 1 sign bit + 3 exponent bits + 2 mantissa bits) is placed into the lower 6 bits (positions 5 through 0) of the 8-bit output. The two most significant bits (positions 7 and 6) of the 8-bit output are filled with zeros (zero-extension). The internal field ordering and bit assignments of S, E3, and M2 are preserved exactly during decompression.

SUMMARY OF FIELD MAPPING:
Source 6-bit:  [S][E3][E2][E1][M1][M0]  at positions 5-0
Output 8-bit:  [0][0][S][E3][E2][E1][M1][M0]  at positions 7-0


Figure 197 Individual decompression from 6-bit to 8-bit for E3M2 type



[Figure: tcgen05-decompression-6b8b-individual2]
CONCEPT:
This figure illustrates the bit-level expansion process for decompressing a 6-bit floating point value (E2M3 format) into an 8-bit floating point value. It shows how the individual bit fields are mapped and zero-extended during decompression.

LAYOUT AND STRUCTURE:

The figure contains three horizontal rows, each representing a bit field layout:

ROW 1 - Bit Position Reference Bar (top row):
A row of 8 numbered boxes showing bit positions from left to right:
7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0
These are bit index labels (most significant bit 7 on the left, least significant bit 0 on the right).

ROW 2 - 6-bit Source Format (E2M3, middle row):
This row shows the 6-bit input format aligned to the right portion of the 8-bit field (bits 5 down to 0). It contains three colored fields:
- S (Sign bit): 1 bit wide, green/teal background, aligned at bit position 5
- E2 (Exponent, 2 bits): blue/purple background, spanning bit positions 4 and 3
- M3 (Mantissa, 3 bits): yellow/tan background, spanning bit positions 2, 1, and 0

The 6-bit source occupies positions 5 through 0 (the rightmost 6 positions of the 8-bit reference bar). Positions 7 and 6 are not covered by this row (the row starts at bit 5).

ROW 3 - 8-bit Destination Format (bottom row):
This row shows the full 8-bit output format. It contains:
- 0: a zero-filled bit at position 7 (white/empty background)
- 0: a zero-filled bit at position 6 (white/empty background)
- S (Sign bit): 1 bit wide, green/teal background, at bit position 5
- E2 (Exponent, 2 bits): blue/purple background, spanning bit positions 4 and 3
- M3 (Mantissa, 3 bits): yellow/tan background, spanning bit positions 2, 1, and 0

DATA FLOW AND MAPPING:
- The 6-bit E2M3 source value occupies bits [5:0] of the 8-bit output.
- The two most significant bits (positions 7 and 6) of the 8-bit output are filled with zeros (zero-extension).
- The Sign (S), Exponent (E2), and Mantissa (M3) fields are preserved in their relative positions during decompression.
- No bit reordering occurs; the fields map directly from the 6-bit source to the lower 6 bits of the 8-bit destination.

FIELD SUMMARY:
Field | Bits in source | Bits in destination | Color
S     | 1 bit (bit 5)  | 1 bit (bit 5)       | Green/teal
E2    | 2 bits (4-3)   | 2 bits (4-3)        | Blue/purple
M3    | 3 bits (2-0)   | 3 bits (2-0)        | Yellow/tan
Zero  | N/A            | 2 bits (7-6)        | White (value 0)

##### 9.7.16.9.2. [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: `tcgen05.cp`](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-cp)

`tcgen05.cp`

Initiates an asynchronous copy operation from shared memory to the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory).

Syntax

```
tcgen05.cp.cta_group.shape{.multicast}{.dst_fmt.src_fmt} [taddr], s-desc;

.cta_group = { .cta_group::1, .cta_group::2 }
.src_fmt   = { .b6x16_p32 , .b4x16_p64 }
.dst_fmt   = { .b8x16 }
.shape     = { .128x256b, .4x256b, .128x128b, .64x128b**, .32x128b*** }
.multicast = { .warpx2::02_13** , .warpx2::01_23**, .warpx4*** }
```

Description

Instruction `tcgen05.cp` initiates an asynchronous copy operation from shared memory to the
location specified by the address operand `taddr` in the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory).

The 64-bit register operand `s-desc` is the matrix descriptor which represents the source
matrix in the shared memory that needs to be copied. The format of the matrix descriptor is
described in [Matrix Descriptors](#tcgen05-matrix-descriptors).

The `.shape` qualifier indicates the dimension of data to be copied as described in the
[Data Movement Shape](#tcgen05-data-movement-shape).

Qualifier `.cta_group` specifies the number of CTAs whose [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) is
accessed when a single thread of a single CTA executes the `tcgen05.cp` instruction.
When `.cta_group::1` is specified, the data is copied into the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory)
of the current CTA. When `.cta_group::2` is specified, the data is copied into the
[Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) of both the current and the [peer CTAs](#tcgen05-peer-cta).

All `tcgen05` instructions within a kernel must specify the same value for the `.cta_group`
qualifier.

When the qualifiers `.dst_fmt` and `.src_fmt` are specified, the data is decompressed
from the source format `.src_fmt` in the shared memory to the destination format
`.dst_fmt` in [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) by the copy operation. The details of source
and the destination formats as specified in the section
[Optional Decompression](#tcgen05-optional-decompression).

Some of the `.shape` qualifiers require certain `.multicast` qualifiers.

1. `.64x128b` requires `.warpx2::02_13` or `.warpx2::01_23`
2. `.32x128b` requires `.warpx4`

When the `.multicast` qualifier is specified as either `.warpx2::02_13` or
`.warpx2::01_23` then the data being copied is multicasted into warp pairs and each
warp in the warp pair receive half of the data. Warp pairs are formed as follows:

1. `.warpx2::02_13` : warps 0 and 2 form a pair; warps 1 and 3 form a pair.
2. `.warpx2::01_23` : warps 0 and 1 form a pair; warps 2 and 3 form a pair.

When the `.multicast` modifier is specified as `.warpx4` then the data being
copied is multicasted into all 4 warps.

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* And is supported on following family-specific architectures from PTX ISA version 8.8:

  + `sm_100f` or higher in the same family
  + `sm_101f` or higher in the same family (Renamed to `sm_110f` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_110f` or higher in the same family

Examples

```
tcgen05.cp.cta_group::1.128x256b                 [taddr0], sdesc0;
tcgen05.cp.cta_group::2.128x128b.b8x16.b6x16_p32 [taddr1], sdesc1;
tcgen05.cp.cta_group::1.64x128b.warpx2::02_13    [taddr2], sdesc2;
```

##### 9.7.16.9.3. [Tensorcore 5th Generation Instructions: `tcgen05.shift`](#tcgen05-instructions-tcgen05-shift)

`tcgen05.shift`

Asynchronously shift down the rows of the matrix in the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) for a warp.

Syntax

```
tcgen05.shift.cta_group.down  [taddr];

.cta_group = { .cta_group::1, .cta_group::2 }
```

Description

Instruction `tcgen05.shift` is an asynchronous instruction which initiates the shifting of 32-byte
elements downwards across all the rows, except the last, by one row. The address operand `taddr`
specifies the base address of the matrix in the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) whose rows must
be down shifted.

The lane of the address operand `taddr` must be aligned to 32.

Qualifier `.cta_group` specifies the number of CTAs whose [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory)
is touched when a single thread of a single CTA executes the `tcgen05.shift` instruction.
When `.cta_group::1` is specified, the shift operation is performed in the
[Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) of the current CTA. When `.cta_group::2` is specified,
the shift operation is performed in the [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory) of both the current and the
[peer CTAs](#tcgen05-peer-cta).

All `tcgen05` instructions within a kernel must specify the same value for the `.cta_group`
qualifier.

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.6.

Target ISA Notes

Supported on following architectures:

* `sm_100a`
* `sm_101a` (Renamed to `sm_110a` from PTX ISA version 9.0)
* `sm_103a`
* `sm_110a`

Examples

```
tcgen05.shift.down.cta_group::1 [taddr0];
tcgen05.shift.down.cta_group::2 [taddr1];
```

#### 9.7.16.10. [TensorCore 5th Generation Matrix Multiply and accumulate Operations](#tcgen05-mma)

The 5th generation of TensorCore operations of shape *MxNxK* perform matrix
multiplication and accumulation of the form:

`D = A*B+D`

where:

* the `A` matrix has shape *MxK*, in either Tensor Memory or Shared Memory
* the `B` matrix has shape *KxN*, in Shared Memory of the current CTA and optionally in peer CTA
* the `D` matrix is of the shape *MxN*, in Tensor Memory

Optionally an input predicate can be used to disable the input from the accumulator
matrix and the following operation can be performed as

`D = A*B`

The matrix multiplication and accumulation operations are categorized into various kinds
based on input types and the throughput of the multiplication operation. The following shows the
different kinds of MMA operations that are supported:

1. `f16` : supports `f16` and `bf16` input types.
2. `tf32` : supports `tf32` input types.
3. `f8f6f4` : supports all input combinations of `f8`, `f6` and `f4` types.
4. `i8` : supports signed and unsigned 8-bit integer input types.
5. `mxf8f6f4`/`mxf4` : supports mx-floating points input types.
6. `mxf4nvf4` : supports `mxf4` type and a custom NVIDIA floating-point
   type for inputs where the type of the vector elements is 4 bits and requires a common
   scaling factor to form the complete floating-point type, similar to other mx-types.

Optionally, the 5th generation of TensorCore MMAs support dense and sparse matrix `A`.
[Sparse Matrices](#tcgen05-sparse-matrices) describes the details of the sparse matrices.

Some of the MMA-kinds requires scaling of input matrices from memory to form the matrix
`A` and matrix `B` before performing the MMA operation.
[Block Scaling for tcgen05.mma](#tcgen05-block-scaling) describes the details of the scaling of matrices.

The following table show the various matrices involved in the MMA operations and the memory in
which they can reside:

| Matrix Type | Memory |
| --- | --- |
| `A` | Tensor Memory OR Shared Memory |
| `B` | Shared Memory |
| `D` | Tensor Memory |
| `Sparse Meta Data` |
| `A-Scale` / `B-Scale` |

A sequence of MMA instructions may reuse the same `A` matrix with a sequence of `B`
matrices or may reuse the same `B` matrix with a sequence of `A` matrices.
In these patterns the TensorCore may be able to laod the unchanged matrix once and reuse
it through the sequence without multiple reloads. The `A` or `B` matrices are loaded
into a TensorCore collector buffer (i.e., special cache).

An MMA instruction has an optional `collector` qualifier to specify when an `A` or `B`
matrix is new to the sequence and should be loaded, unchanged within the sequence
and should be reused, or the last use in the sequence and should be discarded.
The `collector` qualifier is used to give the TensorCore permission to reuse a previously
loaded `A` or `B` matrix; however reuse is opportunistic in that the TensorCore may
reload a matrix even when it has permission to reuse that matrix. Thus, the source
memory of an `A` or `B` matrix must not be modified while the MMA instruction using those
matrices has not completed - regardless of `collector` qualifier permissions.

The 5th generation of TensorCore MMAs can be used for general matrix multiplication OR for
convolution operations. In case of convolutions, the activations can be stored in either
matrix `A` or matrix `B` while the weights will be stored in the other matrix.

| Activation Matrix | Weights Matrix | Name of the op | Instruction Name | Collector Buffer Applicability |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `A` | `B` | Activation Stationary | (default `tcgen05.mma`) | Collector buffer is applicable on matrix `A` |
| `B` | `A` | Weights Stationary | `.ws` | Collector buffer is applicable on matrix `B` |

##### 9.7.16.10.1. [Transpose and Negate operations](#tcgen05-transpose-and-negate-operations)

The matrices `A` and `B` can be transposed by specifying the Tranpose `A` Matrix
and Transpose `B` Matrix bits in the instruction descriptor respectively.

The elements of the matrices `A` and `B` can be negated by specifying the Negate
`A` Matrix and Negate `B` Matrix bits in the instruction descriptor respectively.

The support for Transpose and Negate operation for various MMA-Kind are shown in
[Table 51](#tcgen05-transpose-negate-mma-kind).

Table 51 Transpose and Negate operation for various MMA-Kind

| MMA-Kind | Is Transpose A/B supported | Is Negate A/B supported |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `.kind::tf32` | Yes | Yes |
| `.kind::f16` | Yes | Yes |
| `.kind::f8f6f4` | Yes | Yes |
| `.kind::mxf8f6f4` | Yes | Yes |
| `.kind::i8` | Yes | No |
| `.kind::mxf4` | No | Yes |
| `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | No | Yes |

For `.kind::tf32`, the transpose operations on matrices `A` and `B` are supported
only with 128B swizzling mode with 32B swizzle-atomicity.

For all other MMA-Kinds, the transpose operations on matrices `A` and `B` are not supported
on 128B swizzling mode with 32B swizzle-atomicity.

[Table 52](#tcgen05-kind-shapes-8b-transpose-b) shows the valid combinations of N shape with
`.cta_group` qualifier for 8bit transpose B.

Table 52 Various combinations of N shape with .cta_group qualifier for 8bit transpose B

| .cta_group | N shape |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | 16 <= N <= 256, step 16 |
| 2 | 32 <= N <= 256, step 32 |

##### 9.7.16.10.2. [Matrix Layout Organization](#tcgen05-matrix-layout-organization)

[Table 53](#tcgen05-matrices-majorness) describes the major-ness used for different matrices.

Table 53 Major-ness for different matrices

| Matrix | Residing in Memory | Default Major-ness |
| --- | --- | --- |
| D | Tensor Memory | Row-Major |
| A | Tensor Memory |
| Shared Memory | Depends on swizzling mode. Refer [Shared Memory Layout and Swizzling](#tcgen05-shared-memory-layout-swizzling) |
| B | Shared Memory |

##### 9.7.16.10.3. [Valid Combinations of Type-Size, Major-ness and Swizzling](#tcgen05-matrix-layout-organization-valid-comb-type-size-majorness-swizzle)

Table 54 Valid Combinations of Type-Size, Major-ness and Swizzling

| Type-Size | Major-ness | Matrix | Supported Swizzle |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 4-bit, 6-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit | Row | A | All swizzling modes |
| Column | B |
| 8-bit  16-bit | Column (transpose) | A | All except 128B swizzling with 32B atomicity |
| Row (transpose) | B |
| 32-bit | Column (transpose) | A | Only 128B swizzling with 32B atomicity |
| Row (transpose) | B |

##### 9.7.16.10.4. [Packing formats of elements in Tensor and Shared memory](#tcgen05-packing-formats)

###### 9.7.16.10.4.1. [Packing format for matrix D in Tensor Memory](#tcgen05-packing-formats-mat-d)

The sub-word elements of matrix `D` are expected not to be packed within a 32-bit Tensor Memory word.
For example, if the type of elements of the matrix `D` is 16 bits then a Tensor Memory word
would contain a single 16-bit element in its lower 16 bits.

###### 9.7.16.10.4.2. [Packing format for matrix A and B](#tcgen05-packing-formats-mat-a-b)

The 6-bit and 4-bit floating point types have different packing format requirements for
different MMA kinds in both Tensor memory and Shared memory. The requirements are as follows.

###### 9.7.16.10.4.3. [Packing format used for matrix A by `.kind::mxf8f6f4` in Tensor Memory](#tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem)

The individual 4-bit and the 6-bit floating point type elements must be packed in an 8-bit container
in Tensor memory as shown below. The 8-bit containers must be contiguously packed in a 32-bit Tensor
Memory word. For example, if the type of elements of the matrix `A` is 6 bits then 4 consecutive
`A` elements should be packed in one 32-bit Tensor Memory word.

* 4-bit packing format as shown in [Figure 199](#tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem-dig1)

  

[Figure: tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem-dig1]
4-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E2M1

SOURCE DATA TYPE:
- Type: E2M1 (floating point with 2 exponent bits and 1 mantissa bit)
- Bit width: 4 bits per element (1 sign bit + 2 exponent bits + 1 mantissa bit = 4 bits)

CONTAINER:
- Container size: 8 bits (1 byte)
- Bit positions labeled 7 (MSB) down to 0 (LSB)

BIT-LEVEL LAYOUT WITHIN THE 8-BIT CONTAINER:

Bit 7: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 6: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 5: S (sign bit of E2M1 element)
Bit 4: E2 (exponent bit, high)
Bit 3: E2 (exponent bit, low)
Bit 2: M1 (mantissa bit)
Bit 1: 0 (padding, fixed zero)
Bit 0: 0 (padding, fixed zero)

FIELD SUMMARY TABLE:
Bits 7-6  | Value: 00       | Role: Upper padding (2 bits, fixed zero)
Bit  5    | Value: S        | Role: Sign bit (green shading)
Bits 4-3  | Value: E2       | Role: 2-bit exponent (blue shading)
Bit  2    | Value: M1       | Role: 1-bit mantissa (yellow/orange shading)
Bits 1-0  | Value: 00       | Role: Lower padding (2 bits, fixed zero)

PACKING DETAILS:
- Only 1 element of E2M1 is stored per byte
- The 4-bit E2M1 value occupies bits 5:2 (centered within the byte)
- 4 padding bits total: 2 upper bits (7:6) and 2 lower bits (1:0), all set to zero
- This is NOT a dense packing; the element is not packed at the LSB or MSB boundary

CONTEXT:
- No explicit memory type (shared/tensor/register) is labeled in the figure
- The figure shows the internal bit field decomposition of a single E2M1 value within an 8-bit container


  Figure 199 4-bit packing format with type E2M1
* 6-bit packing format

  + Type E3M2 as shown in [Figure 200](#tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem-dig2)

    

[Figure: tcgen05-packing-formats-mxf8f6f4-tmem-dig2]
6-BIT PACKING FORMAT WITH TYPE E3M2

SOURCE DATA TYPE
Type: E3M2 (floating point with 3 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits)

##### 9.7.16.10.5. [Data Path Layout Organization](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-organization)

Different MMA variants access the tensor memory with different layout organization.
The following table lists the various layouts:

| M | cta_group | A-Sparsity | Is .ws mode | Datapath organization | Layout ID | Tensor Memory Datapath Lane Alignment |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 32 | ::1 | Either | Yes | 1x4 | [Layout G](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-g) | 0 |
| 64 | ::1 | Either | Yes | 2x2 | [Layout E](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-e) | 0 |
| 64 | ::1 | Either | No | 4x1 (1/2 datapath utilized) | [Layout F](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-f) | 0 or 16 |
| 128 | ::1 | Either | Either | 4x1 | [Layout D](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-d) | 0 |
| 128 | ::2 | Dense | N/A | 2x2 | [Layout B](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-b) | 0 |
| 128 | ::2 | Sparse | N/A | 4x1 (1/2 datapath utilized) | [Layout C](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-c) | 0 or 16 |
| 256 | ::2 | Either | N/A | 4x1 | [Layout A](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-a) | 0 |

The layouts which utilize only half the datapath lanes, i.e.,
[Layout F](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-f) and
[Layout C](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-c), must use the same Tensor Memory
lane alignment across matrices `A`, `D` and the sparsity metadata matrix.

The following shows the warps that can access the Tensor Memory regions via
`tcgen05.ld` / `tcgen05.st` along with the addresses for various Tensor Memory Layouts.

###### 9.7.16.10.5.1. [Layout A (M = 256)](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-a)

Layout organization for M = 256 is shown in [Figure 205](#tcgen05-data-path-layout-a1).

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows a matrix multiplication data layout organization for a warp-cooperative matrix operation (likely wgmma or similar NVIDIA tensor core operation). It describes how rows of the A Matrix (with M=256 rows total) are distributed across warps and CTAs (Cooperative Thread Arrays) in a CTA-Pair configuration. The B Matrix spans columns 0 to N-1 and is shown as a single block across the top.

STRUCTURE:
The layout is a 2D matrix decomposition table with:
- Vertical axis: A Matrix rows (M dimension), divided into 8 row-groups of 32 rows each
- Horizontal axis: B Matrix columns (0 to N-1), shown as a single unified block

TABLE LAYOUT (8 rows x 2 columns):

Row 1: M 0-31     | warp-rank % 4 == 0  + Even CTA in CTA-Pair   [Dark green background]
Row 2: M 32-63    | warp-rank % 4 == 1  + Even CTA in CTA-Pair   [Medium green background]
Row 3: M 64-95    | warp-rank % 4 == 2  + Even CTA in CTA-Pair   [Light green background]
Row 4: M 96-127   | warp-rank % 4 == 3  + Even CTA in CTA-Pair   [Lightest green background]
Row 5: M 128-159  | warp-rank % 4 == 0  + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair    [Dark gold/orange background]
Row 6: M 160-191  | warp-rank % 4 == 1  + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair    [Medium gold/yellow background]
Row 7: M 192-223  | warp-rank % 4 == 2  + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair    [Light yellow background]
Row 8: M 224-255  | warp-rank % 4 == 3  + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair    [Lightest yellow background]

- Total M dimension: 256 rows
- Each row-group covers exactly 32 rows
- 8 total row-groups
- 4 warp-rank groups (warp-rank % 4 == 0, 1, 2, 3)
- 2 CTA types in a CTA-Pair: Even CTA and Odd CTA

ASSIGNMENT PATTERN:
- Rows M 0-127 (first 128 rows) are assigned to the Even CTA in the CTA-Pair
- Rows M 128-255 (second 128 rows) are assigned to the Odd CTA in the CTA-Pair
- Within each 128-row half, the 4 groups of 32 rows are assigned to warps based on warp-rank modulo 4:
  - warp-rank % 4 == 0: first 32 rows of the half (M 0-31 or M 128-159)
  - warp-rank % 4 == 1: second 32 rows of the half (M 32-63 or M 160-191)
  - warp-rank % 4 == 2: third 32 rows of the half (M 64-95 or M 192-223)
  - warp-rank % 4 == 3: fourth 32 rows of the half (M 96-127 or M 224-255)

COLOR CODING:
- Green shades (4 gradations from dark to light): Even CTA in CTA-Pair (M 0-127)
- Gold/Yellow shades (4 gradations from dark to light): Odd CTA in CTA-Pair (M 128-255)
- Within each color family, darker shade = lower warp-rank value (0), lighter shade = higher warp-rank value (3)

LAYOUT ORGANIZATION: M = 128 + .cta_group::2 + Sparse A Matrix

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows the warp-rank and CTA (Cooperative Thread Array) assignment layout for a Sparse A matrix with M=128 rows, used in a matrix multiplication operation with a CTA group size of 2 (cta_group::2). The layout maps M-dimension row ranges to specific warp-ranks and CTA positions within a CTA-Pair.

MATRIX DIMENSIONS:
- A Matrix: 128 rows (M dimension, rows 0 through 127), columns not explicitly bounded
- B Matrix: columns 0 to N-1 (shown as a single block at the top right)

LAYOUT TABLE STRUCTURE:
The A Matrix is divided into 8 horizontal row bands, each spanning 16 rows. Each band is assigned to a specific warp-rank modulo condition and a CTA designation (Even or Odd within a CTA-Pair).

ROW BAND ASSIGNMENTS (top to bottom):

Row Band 1: M 0-15
  Color: Dark green
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 0 + Even CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 2: M 16-31
  Color: Medium green
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 1 + Even CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 3: M 32-47
  Color: Light green
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 2 + Even CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 4: M 48-63
  Color: Pale/yellow-green
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 3 + Even CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 5: M 64-79
  Color: Dark gold/orange-yellow
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 0 + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 6: M 80-95
  Color: Medium yellow-orange
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 1 + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 7: M 96-111
  Color: Light yellow
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 2 + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair

Row Band 8: M 112-127
  Color: Pale yellow
  Assignment: warp-rank % 4 == 3 + Odd CTA in CTA-Pair

ORGANIZATIONAL PATTERN:
- The 128 M-dimension rows are split into two halves: rows 0-63 (Even CTA) and rows 64-127 (Odd CTA).
- Within each half, the 64 rows are further divided into 4 bands of 16 rows each.
- Each 16-row band is assigned to a warp-rank satisfying warp-rank % 4 == 0, 1, 2, or 3 respectively.
- The warp-rank modulo pattern repeats identically for both Even and Odd CTA halves.
- Color coding uses green shades for Even CTA rows and yellow/gold shades for Odd CTA rows, with intensity decreasing as warp-rank index increases within each group.

CTA-PAIR STRUCTURE:
- cta_group::2 means CTAs operate in pairs.
- The Even CTA in the pair handles M rows 0-63.
- The Odd CTA in the pair handles M rows 64-127.
- Each CTA uses 4 warp-ranks (0 through 3 mod 4) to cover its 64 rows, with each warp-rank covering 16 rows.

SUMMARY OF MAPPING:
warp-rank % 4 == 0, Even CTA -> M 0-15
warp-rank % 4 == 1, Even CTA -> M 16-31
warp-rank % 4 == 2, Even CTA -> M 32-47
warp-rank % 4 == 3, Even CTA -> M 48-63
warp-rank % 4 == 0, Odd CTA  -> M 64-79
warp-rank % 4 == 1, Odd CTA  -> M 80-95
warp-rank % 4 == 2, Odd CTA  -> M 96-111
warp-rank % 4 == 3, Odd CTA  -> M 112-127

- Warp-ranks 2 and 3 handle the right N half (columns (N/2) to N-1), for upper and lower M halves respectively

- warp-rank % 4 == 0  corresponds to  B Matrix columns 0 to (N/4)-1
- warp-rank % 4 == 1  corresponds to  B Matrix columns (N/4) to (N/2)-1
- warp-rank % 4 == 2  corresponds to  B Matrix columns (N/2) to (N/4*3)-1
- warp-rank % 4 == 3  corresponds to  B Matrix columns (N/4*3) to N-1

DATA ORGANIZATION:
- The A Matrix (M=32) spans all 32 rows (M 0-31) and is replicated or shared across the full N dimension, but each warp (identified by warp-rank modulo 4) is responsible for a specific quarter of the N dimension.
- The B Matrix is partitioned into 4 equal quarters along the N dimension, each of width N/4.
- The layout is horizontal/row-major in presentation, with the N dimension running left to right.
- The warp rank modulo 4 determines which quarter of the N dimension a given warp processes.
- All 4 warp-rank groups cover the same A Matrix rows (M 0-31), but each operates on a different N/4 slice of the B Matrix.

VISUAL ENCODING:
- Color gradient from dark green (warp-rank % 4 == 0) to progressively lighter greens (warp-rank % 4 == 3) visually distinguishes the four warp groups.
- The "M 0-31" label cell uses a distinct light blue/gray color to differentiate it as a dimension label rather than a data partition.

##### 9.7.16.10.6. [Shared Memory Layout and Swizzling](#tcgen05-shared-memory-layout-swizzling)

If the bit `Transpose A Matrix` / `Transpose B Matrix` in the
[Instruction descriptor](#tcgen05-instruction-descriptor) is 0, then *K-major* is
used for matrix `A` / `B` respectively. If the bit `Transpose A Matrix` in the
[Instruction descriptor](#tcgen05-instruction-descriptor) is 1 then *M-major* is
used for matrix `A`. If the bit `Transpose B Matrix` in the
[Instruction descriptor](#tcgen05-instruction-descriptor) is 1, then *N-major* is
used for matrix `B`.

In a column-major default BLAS library such as cuBLAS, the matrices `A` and `B` with and
without transpose can be classified as either *K-Major* or *M-or-N-Major* as shown in the
following table:

|  | Non-Transposed | Transposed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| A | K-major | M-major |
| B | K-major | N-major |

To avoid confusion with `A`, `B`, `row-major`, `col-major`, `transpose`, and
`non-transpose`, we will use *MN-Major* and *K-Major* throughout this section.

The matrices in the shared memory are made up of one or more “swizzle layout atom”.
The exact layout of these swizzle atoms depends on the swizzling mode, swizzle-atomicity,
and the leading dimension. The layout of the swizzle are shown in
[Table 55](#tcgen05-smem-swizzle-mode)

Table 55 Layout for swizzle atoms

| Swizzling mode and Swizzle-Atomicity | Leading Dimension | Swizzle atom layout (128b element) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 128B Swizzling with 32B atomicity | M/N | 8x4 |
| – | – |
| 128B Swizzling with 16B atomicity | M/N | 8x8 |
| K | 8x8 |
| 64B Swizzling Mode | M/N | 4x8 |
| K | 8x4 |
| 32B Swizzling Mode | M/N | 2x8 |
| K | 8x2 |
| None | M/N | 1x8 |
| K | 8x1 |

The above shapes are for elements of size 128 bits. For smaller element sizes, the same shapes
would get multiplied along the leading dimension by a factor of `128 / sizeof_bits(Element)`.
For example, 128B MN major swizzle atom would have a shape of (8\*(128/32))x8 = 32x8 for
tf32 tensor core inputs.

Some example Layouts of *MxK* or *KxN* matrices with various swizzling modes, and are in units
of 128b elements as shown by each colored cell as shown in
[Figure 219](#tcgen05-smem-layout-128b-32b-atom-mn),
[Figure 220](#tcgen05-smem-layout-128b-mn),
[Figure 221](#tcgen05-smem-layout-128b-k),
[Figure 222](#tcgen05-smem-layout-64b-mn),
[Figure 223](#tcgen05-smem-layout-64b-k),
[Figure 224](#tcgen05-smem-layout-32b-mn),
[Figure 225](#tcgen05-smem-layout-32b-k),
[Figure 226](#tcgen05-smem-layout-no-swizzle-mn),
[Figure 227](#tcgen05-smem-layout-no-swizzle-k).

MN Major 128B Swizzling with 32B Atomicity

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates a swizzled memory layout pattern used in NVIDIA GPU tensor/matrix operations. Specifically, it shows how data elements are arranged in shared memory using 128-byte swizzling with 32-byte atomicity granularity. The swizzling pattern remaps logical matrix positions to physical memory locations to avoid bank conflicts in shared memory. This is an MN-major layout, meaning the M/N dimension is the contiguous (fast-varying) dimension in memory.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis (left to right): K dimension, labeled "K dim" with a rightward arrow at the top. Columns are indexed 0, 1, 2, 3.
- Vertical axis (top to bottom): M/N dimension, labeled "M/N dim (contiguous)" with a downward arrow on the left side. Rows are indexed 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.

GRID STRUCTURE:
The grid is 8 rows (M/N indices 0-7) by 4 columns (K indices 0-3), giving 32 total cells.

NUMERICAL VALUES IN EACH CELL (row, column -> value):
Row 0: col0=0,  col1=10, col2=20, col3=30
Row 1: col0=1,  col1=11, col2=21, col3=31
Row 2: col0=2,  col1=8,  col2=22, col3=28
Row 3: col0=3,  col1=9,  col2=23, col3=29
Row 4: col0=4,  col1=14, col2=16, col3=26
Row 5: col0=5,  col1=15, col2=17, col3=27
Row 6: col0=6,  col1=12, col2=18, col3=24
Row 7: col0=7,  col1=13, col2=19, col3=25

COLOR CODING:
Each cell is colored with one of four colors (purple/blue, yellow/cream, green, red/pink). The colors represent which 32-byte atomic unit or swizzle group the element belongs to. The color pattern is not uniform across rows and columns — it is deliberately permuted to implement the swizzle pattern. The four colors correspond to the four 32B segments within a 128B swizzle tile.

COLOR ASSIGNMENTS PER CELL:
Row 0: purple, yellow, purple, yellow
Row 1: green,  red,    green,  red
Row 2: yellow, purple, yellow, purple
Row 3: red,    green,  red,    green
Row 4: yellow, green,  purple, yellow  (approximate — swizzle shifts)
Row 5: green,  green,  red,    red
Row 6: yellow, purple, yellow, purple
Row 7: yellow, green,  red,    green

(Note: The exact color per cell encodes the swizzle XOR pattern applied to the physical address.)

KEY OBSERVATIONS ABOUT THE SWIZZLE PATTERN:
1. The numbers 0-31 are distributed across the 32 cells, each appearing exactly once.
2. The values do NOT follow a simple row-major or column-major sequential order. Instead, they are permuted according to the swizzle function.
3. Within each pair of rows (0-1, 2-3, 4-5, 6-7), the K=0 column contains consecutive values (0,1 / 2,3 / 4,5 / 6,7), indicating the M/N dimension is contiguous in the base addressing.
4. The K dimension values are remapped (swizzled) differently for each group of rows, which is the hallmark of XOR-based swizzling to distribute accesses across shared memory banks.
5. The 128B swizzle period covers the full 4-column (K=0..3) width, and the 32B atomicity means swizzle permutations occur in groups corresponding to 32-byte chunks.

PURPOSE:
This layout is used with wgmma (warpgroup matrix multiply-accumulate) or cp.async.bulk (TMA) instructions in NVIDIA PTX to ensure conflict-free shared memory access patterns when loading matrix tiles, by XOR-ing the K-dimension index into the address offset within each 128-byte row.

DATA ORGANIZATION PATTERN

- Along the K dimension, the addressing is swizzled (non-sequential) to avoid shared memory bank conflicts
- Within each group of 4 rows, the K-column offset rotates, implementing the XOR-based swizzle
- The swizzle pattern shifts by one color position per row, creating a diagonal pattern of colors
- Elements 0-7 occupy column 0 sequentially, confirming M/N contiguous layout
- The swizzle maps logical (row, col) to physical memory locations that are 128 bytes apart in the swizzle period

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS
- The numbering within each 4-row, 4-column quadrant follows a column-major then swizzled order
- Left half (K cols 0-3): elements 0-31
- Right half (K cols 4-7): elements 32-63
- Within each half, the swizzle rotates the starting K column per row by 1 position (mod 4), which is the standard 128B XOR swizzle for 4-element (or 4-phase) swizzle width

CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates "K major 128B swizzling" — a memory layout swizzling pattern used in NVIDIA GPU tensor/matrix operations. Swizzling reorders how matrix elements are stored in shared memory to avoid bank conflicts. The K dimension is the contiguous (fast-varying) dimension in memory, and the M/N dimension is the slower-varying dimension. The numbers in each cell represent the physical memory order (swizzled addresses or element indices) of logically-addressed matrix elements.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis: K dim (contiguous) — labeled at the top with an arrow pointing right. Columns are indexed 0 through 7.
- Vertical axis: M/N dim — labeled on the left with an arrow pointing downward. Rows are indexed 0 through 7.
- The grid is 8 columns (K: 0–7) by 8 rows (M/N: 0–7), giving 64 total elements.

GRID CONTENTS (row by row, format is [row, col] = value):

Row 0 (M/N=0): col0=0,  col1=1,  col2=2,  col3=3,  col4=4,  col5=5,  col6=6,  col7=7
Row 1 (M/N=1): col0=9,  col1=8,  col2=11, col3=10, col4=13, col5=12, col6=15, col7=14
Row 2 (M/N=2): col0=18, col1=19, col2=16, col3=17, col4=22, col5=23, col6=20, col7=21
Row 3 (M/N=3): col0=27, col1=26, col2=25, col3=24, col4=31, col5=30, col6=29, col7=28
Row 4 (M/N=4): col0=36, col1=37, col2=38, col3=39, col4=32, col5=33, col6=34, col7=35
Row 5 (M/N=5): col0=45, col1=44, col2=47, col3=46, col4=41, col5=40, col6=43, col7=42
Row 6 (M/N=6): col0=54, col1=55, col2=52, col3=53, col4=50, col5=51, col6=48, col7=49
Row 7 (M/N=7): col0=63, col1=62, col2=61, col3=60, col4=59, col5=58, col6=57, col7=56

COLOR CODING:
Each cell is colored with one of four colors (blue/purple, green, yellow/olive, red/pink). The colors appear to indicate which swizzle group or bank group each element belongs to. The coloring pattern within each row shifts, reflecting the XOR-based swizzle offset applied per row. The four colors correspond to four groups of 16 elements each (0–15, 16–31, 32–47, 48–63), or alternatively to shared memory bank groups.

Color pattern observations per row:
Row 0: blue, green, yellow, red, green, yellow, green, red (approximate — colors cycle with a specific pattern)
The color of each cell corresponds to which 16-element block the stored value falls in:
  Values 0–15: one color group
  Values 16–31: another color group
  Values 32–47: another color group
  Values 48–63: another color group

SWIZZLE PATTERN ANALYSIS:
The swizzle is XOR-based. For each row r and column c, the physical address is computed as:
  physical_col = c XOR swizzle_offset(r)

The swizzle offsets per row (derived from the data):
Row 0: offset = 0  (col 0 → value 0, sequential)
Row 1: offset = 1  (col 0 → 9, col 1 → 8: pairs swapped within groups of 2)
Row 2: offset = 2  (groups of 4 rotated)
Row 3: offset = 3  (groups of 4 reversed)
Row 4: offset = 4  (col 4 → 32, half-row XOR by 4)
Row 5: offset = 5
Row 6: offset = 6
Row 7: offset = 7

Each row r applies an XOR of r to the column index to determine which physical 8-element slot receives which logical element. This is the standard 128-byte swizzle pattern used in NVIDIA shared memory layouts for tensor core operations, where each row's starting column is XOR-shifted by the row index to distribute accesses across different shared memory banks and avoid conflicts.

SIZE CONTEXT:
The 128B in the title refers to 128 bytes per row in shared memory. With 8 columns and 16 bytes per element group (or 8 elements of 16 bytes each = 128 bytes), each row spans exactly 128 bytes, which is the swizzle granularity.

SUMMARY:
The figure shows an 8x8 logical matrix where the value in each cell is the physical (swizzled) memory index of that element. Row 0 is stored sequentially (0–7). Each subsequent row applies an increasing XOR offset to the column index, causing the physical storage order to shift. This eliminates shared memory bank conflicts when multiple threads in a warp access the same column across different rows simultaneously.

This figure illustrates the memory layout pattern for "MN major 32B swizzling" as used in NVIDIA PTX ISA tensor memory operations. It shows how 32-byte swizzling reorders data elements within a 2D matrix tile to optimize memory access patterns and reduce bank conflicts in shared memory.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS
- Horizontal axis (left to right): K dimension, labeled "K dim" with an arrow pointing right. Columns are indexed 0 through 7.
- Vertical axis (top to bottom): M/N dimension, labeled "M/N dim (contiguous)" with an arrow pointing downward. Rows are indexed 0 and 1.

GRID STRUCTURE
The grid is 2 rows by 8 columns, for a total of 16 cells. Each cell contains a single integer value (0 through 15).

CELL VALUES AND LAYOUT
Row 0 (top row), columns 0-7: 0, 2, 4, 6, 9, 11, 13, 15
Row 1 (bottom row), columns 0-7: 1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 10, 12, 14

CELL COLOR CODING (swizzle groupings)
The colors indicate which 32-byte swizzle group each element belongs to. The color pattern is as follows:

Row 0:
- Column 0: Purple/lavender (value 0)
- Column 1: Yellow/lime (value 2)
- Column 2: Purple/lavender (value 4)
- Column 3: Yellow/lime (value 6)
- Column 4: Green (value 9)
- Column 5: Red/salmon (value 11)
- Column 6: Green (value 13)
- Column 7: Red/salmon (value 15)

Row 1:
- Column 0: Green (value 1)
- Column 1: Red/salmon (value 3)
- Column 2: Green (value 5)
- Column 3: Red/salmon (value 7)
- Column 4: Purple/lavender (value 8)
- Column 5: Yellow/lime (value 10)
- Column 6: Purple/lavender (value 12)
- Column 7: Yellow/lime (value 14)

COLOR GROUPINGS SUMMARY
Four distinct colors appear, each used for 4 cells total across the grid:
- Purple/lavender: (row0,col0)=0, (row0,col2)=4, (row1,col4)=8, (row1,col6)=12
- Yellow/lime: (row0,col1)=2, (row0,col3)=6, (row1,col5)=10, (row1,col7)=14

DATA ORGANIZATION PATTERN
The numbering (0-15) represents the logical sequential order of elements. The swizzling reorders them so that elements that are contiguous in the M/N dimension (rows 0 and 1 at the same K column) are interleaved. Specifically, odd-numbered logical elements appear in row 1 and even-numbered in row 0, but the K-column placement is shifted/swizzled between the two rows. The color grouping shows that the swizzle pattern shifts by 4 columns between row 0 and row 1, meaning the 32-byte swizzle XORs the column index based on the row index to distribute data across different memory banks.

K Major 32B Swizzling - Complete Description

CONCEPT OVERVIEW
This figure illustrates a "K major 32B swizzling" pattern used in NVIDIA GPU shared memory layout for matrix operations. Swizzling is a technique to remap memory addresses to avoid bank conflicts in shared memory. This specific pattern shows how 16 data elements (numbered 0-15) are arranged in a 2D tile with swizzled (permuted) addressing.

AXES AND DIMENSIONS
- Horizontal axis: K dim (contiguous) - labeled at top right with a rightward arrow. This is the K dimension, which is the contiguous (fast-varying) dimension in memory.
- Vertical axis: M/N dim - labeled at left with a downward arrow. This is the M or N dimension (the non-contiguous dimension).
- Column indices (K dim): 0 and 1 (two columns)
- Row indices (M/N dim): 0 through 7 (eight rows)

GRID LAYOUT
The grid is 8 rows by 2 columns, containing 16 cells total. Each cell holds a single integer value (0-15). The values are NOT stored in sequential row-major or column-major order; they are swizzled (permuted) to avoid shared memory bank conflicts.

CELL VALUES BY POSITION (row, column):
Row 0: col 0 = 0,  col 1 = 1
Row 1: col 0 = 2,  col 1 = 3
Row 2: col 0 = 4,  col 1 = 5
Row 3: col 0 = 6,  col 1 = 7
Row 4: col 0 = 9,  col 1 = 8
Row 5: col 0 = 11, col 1 = 10
Row 6: col 0 = 13, col 1 = 12
Row 7: col 0 = 15, col 1 = 14

COLOR CODING OF CELLS
Each cell is color-coded. The colors appear to represent which swizzle group or bank group each element belongs to. The colors observed are:
- Blue/purple (medium blue): row 0 col 0 (value 0), row 2 col 0 (value 4), row 4 col 1 (value 8), row 6 col 1 (value 12)
- Green (light green): row 0 col 1 (value 1), row 2 col 1 (value 5), row 4 col 0 (value 9), row 6 col 0 (value 13)
- Yellow/lime: row 1 col 0 (value 2), row 3 col 1 (value 7), row 5 col 1 (value 10), row 7 col 1 (value 14)
- Pink/red (salmon): row 1 col 1 (value 3), row 3 col 0 (value 6), row 5 col 0 (value 11), row 7 col 0 (value 15)

SWIZZLING PATTERN ANALYSIS
- Rows 0-3: Elements 0-7 are stored in sequential order (0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7) reading left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
- Rows 4-7: The column order is swapped/XORed. In row 4, col 0 = 9 and col 1 = 8 (reversed from sequential). In row 5, col 0 = 11, col 1 = 10 (reversed). In row 6, col 0 = 13, col 1 = 12 (reversed). In row 7, col 0 = 15, col 1 = 14 (reversed).
- This indicates a swizzle where the column index is XORed with a function of the row index (specifically, rows 4-7 have their two columns swapped relative to rows 0-3), consistent with a 32-byte swizzle pattern applied to avoid bank conflicts.

KEY TAKEAWAY FOR PROGRAMMERS
In K-major layout with 32B swizzling, the physical memory location of each element is determined by XORing the column (K) index with a value derived from the row (M/N) index. The first half of rows (0-3) store elements in natural order within each row, while the second half of rows (4-7) store elements with the column positions swapped. This remapping distributes accesses across different shared memory banks, eliminating bank conflicts during matrix tile loads.

##### 9.7.16.10.7. [Block Scaling for `tcgen05.mma`](#tcgen05-block-scaling)

The `tcgen05.mma` instructions with the following `.kind` qualifier:

* `.kind::mxf8f6f4`
* `.kind::mxf4`
* `.kind::mxf4nvf4`

perform matrix multiplication with block scaling. This operation has the following form:

`(A * scale_A)  * (B * scale_B) + D`

where `scale_A` and `scale_B` are matrices residing in [Tensor Memory](#tensor-memory).

For a `scale_A` matrix of shape *M x SFA_N*, each row of matrix `A` is divided into
*SFA_N* number of chunks and each chunk of a row is multiplied with the corresponding
element in the *SF_A* of the same row.

Similarly, for a `scale_B` matrix of shape *SFB_M x N*, each column of matrix `B` is
divided into the *SFB_M* number of chunks and each chunk of a column is multiplied with
the corresponding element in the *SF_B* of the same column.

Scale factors for `A` and `B` matrices need to be duplicated to all 32 lane partitions
of tensor memory.

[Figure 230](#tcgen05-mma-block-scaling) shows an example of `tcgen05.mma` with block scaling of
`scale_vec::2X`.



[Figure: tcgen05-mma-block-scaling]

OVERALL LAYOUT:
The figure is divided into two main spatial regions:

- Lower region: Matrix A (bottom left) with M and K dimensions, scale-A matrix (immediately to the right of Matrix A), scale-B matrix (above Matrix D), and Matrix D (bottom right, output)

   - The matrix is visually divided into two horizontal bands (two rows of roughly equal height), indicating the 2X scale_vec blocking along the K dimension
   - Label: "Matrix B" appears to the right of the rectangle

2. MATRIX A (lower left, green rectangle):
   - Color: Green
   - Dimensions labeled: M (vertical, shown with a double-headed arrow on the left side) and K (horizontal, shown with a double-headed arrow along the top)
   - The matrix is visually divided into two vertical bands (two columns of roughly equal width), indicating the 2X scale_vec blocking along the K dimension
   - Label: "Matrix A" appears below the rectangle

3. SCALE-A MATRIX (immediately to the right of Matrix A, blue narrow vertical rectangle):
   - Color: Blue
   - Appears as a narrow vertical strip divided into two horizontal segments (two cells stacked vertically), corresponding to the 2X scaling blocks along the K dimension of Matrix A
   - Label: "scale-A matrix" appears below the rectangle
   - Width is much smaller than Matrix A, representing one scale factor per block of K elements per row group

4. SCALE-B MATRIX (above Matrix D, blue narrow horizontal rectangle):
   - Color: Blue
   - Appears as a narrow horizontal strip divided into two vertical segments (two cells side by side), corresponding to the 2X scaling blocks along the K dimension of Matrix B
   - Label: "scale-B matrix" appears to the right of the rectangle

5. MATRIX D (lower right, orange/yellow rectangle):

   - Dimensions correspond to M (vertical) x N (horizontal), matching the output of the matrix multiplication
   - Label: "Matrix D" appears inside the rectangle
   - No internal divisions shown

DIMENSIONAL RELATIONSHIPS:
- K dimension: shared between Matrix A (horizontal extent) and Matrix B (vertical extent) — this is the contraction dimension
- M dimension: vertical extent of Matrix A and Matrix D
- N dimension: horizontal extent of Matrix B and Matrix D
- The 2X in scale_vec::2X is reflected by the two visible divisions in both Matrix A (two vertical halves along K) and Matrix B (two horizontal halves along K), meaning the K dimension is split into 2 blocks, each with its own scale factor

SCALE VECTOR (2X) INTERPRETATION:
- scale_vec::2X means each scaling block covers K/2 elements along the K dimension
- Scale-A matrix has 2 scale values per row group of Matrix A (one per K/2 block)
- Scale-B matrix has 2 scale values per column group of Matrix B (one per K/2 block)
- The narrow blue rectangles each show exactly 2 segments, confirming the 2X blocking

DATA FLOW:
Matrix A (M x K, green) and Matrix B (K x N, green) are multiplied together. Before multiplication, each K/2-sized block of A rows is scaled by the corresponding entry in scale-A matrix, and each K/2-sized block of B columns is scaled by the corresponding entry in scale-B matrix. The result is accumulated into Matrix D (M x N, orange).

###### 9.7.16.10.7.1. [Valid combinations of scale_vectorsize with types and MMA-Kind](#tcgen05-mma-scale-valid-vec-size)

The shape of *scale_A* and *scale_B* matrices depend on the `.scale_vectorsize` as shown in
[Table 56](#tcgen05-mma-scale-valid-comb).

Table 56 Valid combinations of scale_vectorsize and shapes

| .scale_vectorsize | .kind::\* | K | Shape of scale_A | Shape of scale_B |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `.scale_vec::1X` | `.kind::mxf8f6f4` | All supported values of K | M x 1 | 1 x N |
| `.scale_vec::2X` | `.kind::mxf4`, `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | All supported values of K | M x 2 | 2 x N |
| `.scale_vec::4X` | `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | All supported values of K | M x 4 | 4 x N |
| `.block16` | `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | K = 96 | M x 6 | 6 x N |
| All supported values of K except 96 | M x 4 | 4 x N |
| `.block32` | `.kind::mxf4`, `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | K = 96 | M x 3 | 3 x N |
| All supported values of K except 96 | M x 2 | 2 x N |
| `.kind::mxf8f6f4` | All supported values of K | M x 1 | 1 x N |

The valid combination of the exact element types and the `.scale_vectorsize` are listed in
[Table 57](#tcgen05-mma-scale-valid-comb-detail).

Table 57 Valid combinations of scale_vectorsize with types and MMA-Kind

| .kind::\* | Element Data Type | Scale Data Type | .scale_vectorsize |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `.kind::mxf8f6f4` | E4M3, E5M2, E2M3 E3M2, E2M1 | UE8M0 | `.scale_vec::1X` / `.block32` |
| `.kind::mxf4` | E2M1 | UE8M0 | `.scale_vec::2X` / `.block32` |
| `.kind::mxf4nvf4` | E2M1 | UE8M0 | `.scale_vec::2X` / `.block32`, `.scale_vec::4X` / `.block16` |
| E2M1 | UE4M3 | `.scale_vec::4X` / `.block16` |

New `.blockN` qualifiers are aliases for `.scale_vec::NX` qualifiers as:

* `.block32` is alias for `.scale_vec::1X` or `.scale_vec::2X`
  based on `.kind` and K dimension
* `.block16` is alias for `.scale_vec::4X`

###### 9.7.16.10.7.2. [Scale Factor A ID](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a)

The value of the scale factor `A ID` selects the sub-columns in the Tensor Memory to
form the scale factor `A` matrix, which is used to scale the matrix `A`.

The following shows the scale factor matrix layout for various scale vector sizes:

###### 9.7.16.10.7.2.1. [Layout of the Scale Factor A Matrix for scale_vec::1X/block32 with K=32/K=64](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-layout-1x)

There is one scale factor per row of the `A` matrix with block size as 32 and the scale factor must be provided in
1-byte aligned sub-column of the Tensor Memory. *SFA_ID* specifies the byte offset in the
Tensor Memory word that must be used for the scale factor matrix.
[Figure 231](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-1x-dig) shows which sub-columns get selected for
different values of *SFA_ID*.



[Figure: tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-1x-dig]
LAYOUT OF SCALE FACTOR A MATRIX WITH scale_vec::1X/block32 WITH K=32/K=64

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows the memory layout of a scale factor A matrix used in NVIDIA PTX matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) operations. It maps warp lanes (0-31) to columns and bit-field positions within a 32-bit register, showing how scale factor elements are distributed across threads.

DIMENSIONS AND STRUCTURE:

Rows: 32 lanes (Lane 0 through Lane 31), labeled on the left side.
Columns: 4 groups (Column X, Column X+1, Column X+2, Column X+3), each subdivided into 4 bit-field ranges.

Each column group has 4 sub-columns with bit-field ranges:
- [0:7]
- [8:15]
- [16:23]
- [24:31]

Total sub-columns: 4 groups x 4 bit-fields = 16 sub-columns.

Below the main table, a row of indices 0, 1, 2, 3 appears under the Column X group, indicating register or word indices 0 through 3.

COLOR CODING (SFA ID):
Four colors identify four Scale Factor A IDs:
- Green: SFA ID = 00
- Teal/Cyan: SFA ID = 01
- Yellow: SFA ID = 10
- Orange/Salmon: SFA ID = 11

The color pattern repeats in groups of 4 lanes:
- Lanes 0-3: green, teal, yellow, orange (SFA ID 00, 01, 10, 11)

- Lanes 28-31: green, teal, yellow, orange

ELEMENT NAMING CONVENTION:
Each cell contains an element named "Mn SF0" where:
- n is the matrix row index (M number)
- SF0 indicates scale factor 0

###### 9.7.16.10.7.2.2. [Layout of the Scale Factor A Matrix for scale_vec::2X/block32 with K=64/K=128](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-layout-2x)

There are two scale factors per row of the `A` matrix with block size as 32 and the scale factor must be provided in
2-byte aligned sub-column of the Tensor Memory. *SFA_ID* specifies the half word offset in the
Tensor Memory word that must be used for the scale factor matrix.
[Figure 232](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-2x-dig) shows which sub-columns gets selected for different
values of *SFA_ID*.

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows the memory layout of a scale factor matrix A (SFA) used in NVIDIA PTX matrix multiply-accumulate operations. The layout is organized as a 2D grid mapping 32 lanes (rows) to multiple columns, with each cell containing scale factor identifiers for specific matrix tiles.

DIMENSIONS AND STRUCTURE:

Rows: 32 lanes, labeled Lane 0 through Lane 31 (vertical axis, left side)
Columns: 4 column groups labeled Column X, Column X+1, Column X+2, Column X+3 (horizontal axis, top)
Each column group is subdivided into 4 byte-range sub-columns: [0:7], [8:15], [16:23], [24:31]
Total sub-columns: 16 (4 groups x 4 sub-columns each)
Bottom axis labels: 0, 1, 2, 3 (under Column X sub-columns)

COLOR CODING (Legend at bottom):
- Light green cells: SFA ID = 00
- Teal/dark cyan cells: SFA ID = 10

COLOR PATTERN:
Within each column group, the 4 sub-columns alternate in pairs:
- Sub-columns [0:7] and [8:15]: light green (SFA ID = 00)
- Sub-columns [16:23] and [24:31]: teal (SFA ID = 10)
This pattern repeats identically across all four column groups (X, X+1, X+2, X+3).

CELL NAMING CONVENTION:
Each cell contains a label of the form: M{n} SF{s}
Where:
- M{n} = matrix row/tile index
- SF{s} = scale factor index, either SF0 or SF1

SF0 appears in sub-columns [0:7] and [16:23]
SF1 appears in sub-columns [8:15] and [24:31]

###### 9.7.16.10.7.2.3. [Layout of the Scale Factor A Matrix for scale_vec::4X/block16 with K=64/K=128](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-layout-4x)

There are four scale factors per row of the `A` matrix with block size as 16 and the scale factor must be provided in
4-byte aligned sub-column of the Tensor Memory. The *SFA_ID* value must be 0 and this specifies
that all of the columns (in green) will be used for the scale factor matrix.
[Figure 233](#tcgen05-mma-scale-factor-a-4x-dig) shows which sub-columns gets selected for different
values of *SFA_ID*.

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows the memory layout of a scale factor matrix A (SFA) used in NVIDIA tensor core operations. The layout is identified as SFA ID = 00. It maps 32 lanes (warp lanes 0-31) across 4 columns (Column X through Column X+3), with each column subdivided into 4 byte-range fields.

DIMENSIONS AND STRUCTURE:

Rows: 32 lanes (Lane 0 through Lane 31)
Columns: 4 logical columns labeled Column X, Column X+1, Column X+2, Column X+3
Each column is subdivided into 4 byte fields: [0:7], [8:15], [16:23], [24:31]
Total fields per row: 4 columns x 4 byte fields = 16 fields per lane
Bottom index labels: 0, 1, 2, 3 (under Column X's four sub-fields)

COLUMN STRUCTURE:
Each of the 4 columns (X, X+1, X+2, X+3) contains 4 byte-range sub-columns:
  [0:7]    - bits/bytes 0 to 7
  [8:15]   - bits/bytes 8 to 15
  [16:23]  - bits/bytes 16 to 23
  [24:31]  - bits/bytes 24 to 31

NAMING CONVENTION FOR CELLS:
Each cell contains a matrix index M# followed by a scale factor index SF#.
Format: M{matrix_row} SF{scale_factor_index}
SF index ranges from SF0 to SF3 within each row across the four byte sub-columns of a given column.

- Columns: 4 groups labeled Column X, Column X+1, Column X+2, Column X+3 (horizontal axis)
- Per-byte sub-columns: 16 total, numbered 0 through 15 (bottom axis)
- Each column group contains 4 byte ranges: [0:7], [8:15], [16:23], [24:31]
- Total byte positions per column group: 32 bits (4 bytes)
- SFA_ID = 00 (indicated by green legend box at bottom)

COLUMN STRUCTURE:
Each of the 4 columns (X, X+1, X+2, X+3) is subdivided into 4 byte sub-ranges:
  [0:7]    = bytes 0-7   (sub-columns 0,4,8,12 respectively)
  [8:15]   = bytes 8-15  (sub-columns 1,5,9,13 respectively)
  [16:23]  = bytes 16-23 (sub-columns 2,6,10,14 respectively)
  [24:31]  = bytes 24-31 (sub-columns 3,7,11,15 respectively)

The [24:31] sub-columns appear empty (no data entries) in all four column groups, indicating those byte positions are unused or reserved.

COLOR CODING:
- Light green cells: Column X and Column X+2 data (M0-M31 SF0/SF1/SF2 and M64-M95 SF0/SF1/SF2)
- Dark green cells: Column X+1 and Column X+3 data (M32-M63 SF0/SF1/SF2 and M96-M127 SF0/SF1/SF2)

LAYOUT OF SCALE FACTOR A MATRIX WITH BLOCK32, K=96, SFA_ID=11

OVERVIEW:
This figure shows the memory layout of a scale factor matrix A (SFA) used in NVIDIA tensor core operations. The layout maps matrix elements to specific lanes and byte positions within a structured register/memory format. The layout uses block32 organization with K=96 and SFA_ID=11.

OVERALL DIMENSIONS:
- Horizontal axis: 32 per-byte sub-columns (numbered 0 through 31)
- Vertical axis: 32 lanes (Lane 0 through Lane 31)
- Total grid: 32 lanes x 32 sub-columns = 1024 cells
- The 32 sub-columns are grouped into 8 logical columns: Column X, X+1, X+2, X+3, X+4, X+5, X+6, X+7
- Each logical column spans 4 per-byte sub-columns

COLUMN GROUPING:
Each of the 8 logical columns (X through X+7) is subdivided into 4 byte-range sub-columns:
- Sub-columns [0:7], [8:15], [16:23], [24:31] within each logical column

Mapping of logical columns to per-byte sub-column indices:
- Column X:   sub-columns 0, 1, 2, 3
- Column X+1: sub-columns 4, 5, 6, 7
- Column X+2: sub-columns 8, 9, 10, 11
- Column X+3: sub-columns 12, 13, 14, 15
- Column X+4: sub-columns 16, 17, 18, 19
- Column X+5: sub-columns 20, 21, 22, 23
- Column X+6: sub-columns 24, 25, 26, 27
- Column X+7: sub-columns 28, 29, 30, 31

SFA_ID INDICATOR:
A highlighted box at the bottom of the figure (orange/salmon color) marks sub-columns 9-11 (within Column X+2, specifically the [8:15] and [16:23] byte ranges) as "SFA ID = 11".

ELEMENT NAMING CONVENTION:
Each populated cell contains an element label of the form:
  M[row_index] SF[scale_factor_index]

Where:
- M[row_index] is a matrix row index (M0 through M127)
- SF[scale_factor_index] is a scale factor group index (SF0, SF1, or SF2)
